lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market independent manufacturers supplying specialty, industrial, stationary, motive-power, reserve-power, replacement, and configured battery cells, packs, or systems with repeat external customers and support needs.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive vehicle plants, commodity consumer dry-cell megaplants, large integrated gigafactories, shells, non-control financing vehicles, firms outside the EBITDA band, pure distributors, recyclers without qualifying manufacturing, and project developers that do not manufacture batteries.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Specialized batteries and systems are sold through repeat OEM, distributor, fleet, defense, medical, data-center, telecom, utility, and industrial relationships, with replacement cycles, configuration, chargers, monitoring, spares, and aftermarket support.
lens.deviation_from_default: Narrowed because the NAICS code combines global-scale commodity EV and consumer cell plants with LMM specialty, industrial, stationary, and replacement manufacturers whose customers, capital intensity, transferability, and pricing are fundamentally different.

a.bridge: BLS measures the broader NAICS 335 occupation mix, not wage-weighted tasks in the narrowed battery lens. I map electrode or component handling, assembly, formation, testing, maintenance, and hazardous-material operations to low substitution, while assigning higher exposure to formulation and design assistance, process analytics, vision inspection, yield diagnosis, scheduling, procurement, documentation, sales support, and administration. The proxy may understate R&D intensity or overstate exposure where validated chemistry and safety controls bind.
a.rationale: Battery manufacturing combines physical, safety-sensitive production with data-rich testing and engineering. AI can reduce analysis, inspection, planning, documentation, and indirect labor but cannot replace most material processing, assembly, formation, and accountable release.
a.change_evidence: A lens-specific occupational wage mix and plant time studies separating R&D, process engineering, direct production, formation/testing, quality, maintenance, supply chain, commercial, and administrative work would materially refine exposure.
caveats: The frozen labor ratio is an ancestor-level proxy and may not fit specialty battery operations.

rho.rationale: Process monitoring, defect detection, formation-data analysis, maintenance, scheduling, demand planning, document work, and engineering assistance are implementable within five years. Limited LMM datasets, chemistry drift, traceability, validation, fire risk, export controls, customer qualification, and liability constrain autonomous decisions.
rho.change_evidence: Audited LMM deployments reporting yield, scrap, formation time, false rejects, downtime, engineering hours, safety events, traceability, and customer qualification outcomes would materially move implementation.
caveats: Manufacturing-wide adoption surveys are not battery-specific.
Rapid chemistry and equipment changes can make historical training data stale.

e.rationale: Independent specialty, motive, reserve, medical, defense, and industrial battery firms can fit the lens, but much sector capacity belongs to captive vehicle ventures, global manufacturers, commodity plants, or capital-intensive projects outside the band. Transferability also depends on IP, permits, customer approvals, and technical teams.
e.change_evidence: A verified US firm census by chemistry, application, ownership, plant scale, manufacturing content, customer qualification, permits, and normalized EBITDA would materially change eligibility.
caveats: The frozen dataset explicitly provides no defensible LMM firm count, so this share cannot be converted into a target count.
NAICS consolidation under 335910 increases business-model heterogeneity.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year sale or transfer plans among US employer-business owners across industries, with 22% reporting such plans. I reduce the base because qualifying LMM battery manufacturers are capital-, permit-, IP-, and diligence-intensive, and planned exits may become closures or asset sales. Strategic demand can create upside. The proxy may overstate completed transfers or understate technology acquisitions.
s5.rationale: Succession and strategic technology interest can create transactions, but missing denominator data, hazardous-material diligence, customer qualification, capital needs, IP dependence, and chemistry obsolescence reduce confidence and completion probability.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of completed US specialty and industrial battery control transactions from PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, battery advisers, and trade publications, divided by a verified eligible-firm denominator, would materially change the probability.
caveats: Gallup is cross-industry and measures plans rather than completed transfers.
No defensible dataset firm count exists for this record.

q.rationale: Qualification, proprietary chemistry or pack design, safety performance, installed systems, and replacement compatibility can preserve savings in specialty applications. Rapid cost decline, customer scale, competitive chemistry, Asian supply, tendering, and learning-curve expectations pass substantial gains through over five years.
q.change_evidence: LMM customer contracts and price histories showing indexation, annual cost-downs, warranty sharing, qualification costs, tender cadence, aftermarket pricing, and post-yield-improvement margins would materially change retention.
caveats: Specialty pricing differs sharply from commodity lithium-ion cells and lead-acid products.
IEA's global cost declines may not map directly to qualified niche batteries.

d5.bridge: IEA measures global battery deployment across EV and stationary markets, not constant-quality US demand for LMM specialty manufacturers. Global battery demand exceeded 1.5 TWh in 2025 after growing over 35%, storage additions rose 40%, and data-center UPS capacity additions rose 30%. I substantially temper these rates for the narrowed US specialty lens, which excludes most EV gigafactory volume and may lose share to global suppliers. The proxy may overstate accessible growth but understate selected defense, data-center, and industrial niches.
d5.rationale: Data centers, telecom, grid storage, warehouse electrification, defense, aerospace, medical devices, industrial vehicles, resilience, and replacement cycles support growth. Chemistry shifts, oversupply, import competition, policy changes, customer insourcing, and price compression widen the range.
d5.change_evidence: US constant-dollar shipments and order data split by specialty, motive, reserve, stationary, UPS, defense, medical, and replacement applications, plus customer forecasts, would materially change the ratio.
caveats: Global deployment is dominated by products and manufacturers outside the lens.
Capacity and energy measures are not constant-quality unit demand and improve with energy density.

o.rationale: Customers still require an accountable producer for physical electrochemical storage, safety design, pack integration, testing, traceability, warranty, and replacement compatibility. Software improves management but does not eliminate the battery; standardized imported cells or customer pack assembly can displace some lens operators.
o.change_evidence: Customer make-versus-buy studies and product roadmaps measuring direct cell sourcing, in-house pack assembly, battery-as-a-service, alternative storage chemistries, and standardized module adoption would materially change operator requirement.
caveats: Alternative storage technologies can displace batteries in some applications.
A shift to a global supplier can remove an LMM operator without eliminating accountable manufacturing.

narrative.executive_view: Battery manufacturing has strong demand and useful data-rich AI applications, but the NAICS code is exceptionally heterogeneous and the frozen dataset has no defensible LMM firm count. The coherent specialty and industrial slice remains physical, safety-critical, capital-intensive, and exposed to rapid chemistry and price change.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can improve process control, defect detection, formation analysis, yield diagnosis, predictive maintenance, scheduling, supply planning, engineering, and documentation. Material processing, assembly, testing, qualification, traceability, and safety release remain operator-led.
narrative.value_capture: Niche qualifications, proprietary designs, replacement compatibility, and service can retain gains. Fast global learning curves, powerful customers, import competition, chemistry shifts, and expected cost-downs share much of the benefit.
narrative.firm_availability: Specialty and industrial firms may qualify, but captive ventures, gigafactories, commodity plants, and global strategics dominate capacity. With no defensible frozen firm count, availability cannot be translated into a target quantity.
narrative.demand_durability: Storage, UPS, telecom, material handling, defense, aerospace, medical, resilience, and replacement support growth. Global battery demand is expanding rapidly, though most growth lies outside the narrowed LMM slice and is vulnerable to policy, imports, and chemistry shifts.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The missing firm denominator is the central availability weakness. Other risks include fire and warranty liability, hazardous materials, permits, export controls, mineral supply, price collapse, obsolescence, customer qualification, capital needs, and global competition.
narrative.principal_driver: Expanding electrification, storage, data-center, industrial, and specialty applications create durable physical demand and rich process data.
narrative.principal_weakness: No defensible LMM firm count exists, and rapidly changing chemistry, capital intensity, and global cost competition can overwhelm AI gains.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: Battery Manufacturing
sources.supports: Defines 335910 broadly across primary and storage batteries, including lead-acid, lithium, rechargeable, and primary cells.

sources.title: Electrical Equipment, Appliance, and Component Manufacturing: NAICS 335
sources.supports: Provides broader-sector evidence on assembly, inspection, production, and supervisory occupations.

sources.title: 2026 Roadmap on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning for Smart Manufacturing
sources.supports: Documents industrial AI opportunities and data, integration, reliability, explainability, and safety barriers.

sources.title: Shaping the AI-Powered Factory of the Future
sources.supports: Reports limited formal operational AI strategy adoption and significant data and legacy-system barriers.

sources.title: EnerSys Fiscal 2025 Annual Report
sources.supports: Describes industrial, motive, specialty, UPS, telecom, data-center, utility, defense, medical, and aftermarket battery applications and support to over 10,000 customers.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Reports 22% of US employer-business owners planned a sale, transfer, or public offering within five years.

sources.title: Global Critical Minerals Outlook 2026: Market Overview
sources.supports: Reports global battery demand grew over 35% in 2025 and surpassed 1.5 TWh.

sources.title: Global Energy Review 2026: Battery Storage
sources.supports: Reports 108 GW of 2025 battery-storage additions, up 40%, and data-center UPS additions rising 30% to 45 GW.
